shot.subject: water
shot.notes: the lake at midday, a sheet of moving light in the sculpted land

segments.text: Every world needs a mirror. This is the garden's — a sheet of restless silver poured into the lowest lines of the Gardener's sculpture, reflecting the sky back at itself and hiding a nation underneath.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: the surface only: ripples interleaving, light fracturing and mending

segments.text: It is the most watchable surface in the garden, and the least understood by those who watch it.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: skimming flight just above the surface, reflections streaming past

segments.text: The water.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: macro on wavelets: crests forming, breaking, reforming without pause

segments.text: Begin at the surface, because the surface never begins and never ends. Wind writes on it continuously — small waves riding larger swells, interference stacking pattern on pattern. Mathematicians would call it a superposition. The fish below call it the ceiling. The birds above call it a signal mirror.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: sunlight tracks across the lake in a long, blinding lane

segments.text: When the sun crosses it, the lake answers with a lane of fire. When clouds come, it goes pewter. The water has no color of its own worth mentioning; it wears the sky's wardrobe, an hour behind and upside down.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: from the beach, waves arriving at the shore edge-on, at ant scale enormous

segments.text: And at the shoreline, the mirror becomes machinery. What reads as gentle lapping from above is, at ant height, surf — a rhythmic, world-edge violence that keeps the border honest.

shot.subject: fish
shot.notes: beneath the surface: green light, drifting motes, fish crossing

segments.text: Beneath the mirror, the wardrobe changes entirely. Below is the drowned country of episode nine — cool, layered, and lit through a trembling roof.

shot.subject: whale
shot.notes: the great silhouette slides through the deepest water, then away

segments.text: Its shallows school with fish. Its one deep pocket shelters the giant. Every liter of that country exists because the Gardener cut the ground low enough for the water table to claim — the lake is the sculpture's negative space, made habitable.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: from above: the lake's whole outline, shore to shore, in one frame

segments.text: Seen from height, the water's borders are simply the contour where land surrenders. But borders on this map, we know by now, are opinions — and the water holds the strongest opinions of all.

shot.subject: shore
shot.notes: timelapse feel: the waterline creeps up the beach, pauses, retreats

segments.text: Twice in every lunar day, the whole lake leans ashore. The moon hauls it up the beach — into the grass margins, over the low trails — and then releases it back. We will meet the mechanism itself in a later hour; for now, watch what it does.

shot.subject: shore
shot.notes: water threading between grass stems, claiming ground blade by blade

segments.text: At high water, the garden's strictest border goes soft. Fish nose through drowned meadow. Land-walkers find their roads shortened. The two nations trade territory on a schedule neither of them keeps, set by a light in the sky neither of them has noticed.

shot.subject: shore
shot.notes: following the retreating waterline as wet ground re-enters the land nation

segments.text: Then the water signs the territory back, damp and enriched, and the ants repossess their roads within minutes. It is the most peaceful border war imaginable — fought twice daily, without casualties, for the entire life of the garden.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: full dark: the lake a sheet of black glass scattered with star points

segments.text: The mirror keeps different hours than the meadow. At full dark, when the grass ocean vanishes into itself, the water is still working — the one surface in the garden that can hold starlight, and the only place the two skies of the night, the fixed and the drifting, ever appear in the same frame.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: a firefly's glow crossing low over the surface, doubled by its reflection

segments.text: Watch a firefly cross the shallows and the lake doubles it — one lantern in the air, one swimming inverted beneath. Every night light in the garden is issued its twin here. The fish below see the underside of that show nightly, and give no sign of reviewing it.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: gliding across the black surface as the moon's lane ignites ahead

segments.text: Then the moon clears the rim, and the mirror finds its great subject. The cold lane of light runs shore to shore, rebuilt instantly across every ripple that tries to break it. No surface in the garden repairs itself so fast, because no surface in the garden is so committed to a single image.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: at the shoreline in the dark: small waves arriving, phosphor-pale, unhurried

segments.text: And beneath the shows, the old work continues unadvertised — the waves arriving at the beach all night, metronome-steady, negotiating the border grain by grain. The meadow sleeps and the sky performs, but the water never once stops being water. Of all the residents of this garden, it is the only one with no off-duty hours at all.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: rain striking the surface, each drop a crown and a ring

segments.text: Everything the sky sends eventually lands here. Rain returns to the lake with applause; snow arrives silently and vanishes into it. The water is the garden's collector — of weather, of runoff, of whatever the meadow lets go.

shot.subject: fish
shot.notes: from below: pellets and motes sinking through green light toward waiting mouths

segments.text: And what it collects, it serves. The sinking fallout feeds the fish; the stirred shallows feed the smallest things the fish feed on. The mirror is also a table, set continuously from above.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: evening: the lake goes gold, then rose, holding the sky's last light

segments.text: By dusk it has one duty left — to hold the sunset a few minutes longer than the sky can. The garden's mirror is generous like that.

shot.subject: sky
shot.notes: tilt up from the water's held sunset to the real one above the rim of the world

segments.text: Which raises the question the water has been reflecting all along: what is up there? What lights this mirror, times its days, and paints it twice daily in fire?

shot.subject: sky
shot.notes: the full dome above the garden, clouds burning at the horizon

segments.text: Next: the Sky.
